author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
Another Error Out of His Second Book, to the Effect, that the Soul Deserved to Be Polluted by the Body.
originalBook: Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
title: Another Error Out of His Second Book, to the Effect, that the Soul Deserved to Be Polluted by the Body.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Chapter 6 [VI.]—Another Error Out of His Second Book, to the Effect, that the Soul Deserved to Be Polluted by the Body.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But he is plainly past endurance in what he says in his second book, when he endeavours to solve a very difficult question on original sin, how it belongs to body and soul, if the soul is not derived by parental descent but is breathed afresh by God into a man.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Striving to explain this troublesome and profound point, he thus expresses his view: “Through the flesh the soul fitly recovers its primitive condition, which it seemed to have gradually lost through the flesh, in order that it may begin to be regenerated by the very flesh by which it had deserved to be polluted.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: You observe how this person, having been so bold as to undertake what exceeds his powers, has fallen down such a precipice as to say, that the soul deserved to be defiled by the body; although he could in no wise declare whence it drew on itself this desert, before it put on flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For if it first had from the flesh its desert of sin, let him tell us (if he can) whence (previous to sin) it derived its desert to be contaminated by the flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For this desert, which projected it into sinful flesh to be polluted by it, it of course had either from itself, or, which is much more offensive to our mind, from God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: It certainly could not, previous to its being invested with the flesh, have received from that flesh that ill desert by reason of which it was projected into the flesh, in order to be defiled by it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, if it had the ill desert from its own self, how did it get it, seeing that it did no sin previous to its assumption of flesh?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But if it be alleged that it had the ill desert from God, then, I ask, who could listen to such blasphemy?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Who could permit it to be alleged with impunity?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For the question which arises here, remember, is not, what was the ill desert which adjudged the soul to be condemned after it became incarnate, but what was its ill desert prior to the flesh, which condemned it to the investiture of the flesh, that it might be thereby polluted?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Let him explain this to us, if he can, seeing that he has dared to say that the soul deserved to be defiled by the flesh.
